mnemonics.title: U for Union - 'U' Get Everything
mnemonics.content: Union (∪) means U get ALL elements from both sets. Think: U = U-niverse (everything). The U symbol looks like a cup that holds everything from both sets combined.

mnemonics.title: ∩ Looks Like an Upside-Down U
mnemonics.content: Intersection (∩) is where sets INTER-sect (cross). The ∩ symbol is like an upside-down cup that catches only what falls into BOTH sets - the common area.

mnemonics.title: Complement = Complete Opposite
mnemonics.content: Complement (A' or Ā) is the COMPLETE opposite of A. Remember: Complement = Universal Set - Set A. Think of it as 'NOT A' - everything except what's in A.

mnemonics.title: Difference = Drop It
mnemonics.content: A - B (or A \ B) means take set A and DROP anything that's also in B. Read it as 'A minus B' or 'A without B'.

mnemonics.title: Symmetric Difference = Δ (Delta) = Different
mnemonics.content: A Δ B = (A - B) ∪ (B - A). Remember Delta (Δ) means 'change' or 'difference' - you get elements that are in either set but NOT in both.

mnemonics.title: De Morgan's Laws - 'NOT breaks AND/OR'
mnemonics.content: (A ∪ B)' = A' ∩ B' and (A ∩ B)' = A' ∪ B'. Mnemonic: 'Break the line, change the sign' - when you complement (break the bar), union becomes intersection and vice versa.

mnemonics.title: Commutative Laws - Switch and Flop
mnemonics.content: A ∪ B = B ∪ A and A ∩ B = B ∩ A. Mnemonic: 'Commute = Switch places' - order doesn't matter for union and intersection.

mnemonics.title: Associative Laws - Grouping Doesn't Matter
mnemonics.content: (A ∪ B) ∪ C = A ∪ (B ∪ C) and (A ∩ B) ∩ C = A ∩ (B ∩ C). Mnemonic: 'Associate = Group together' - parenthesis placement doesn't change the result.

acronyms.usage: Use when you want ALL elements from both sets combined, without duplicates. Symbol: ∪
Use when you want ONLY the common elements between sets. Symbol: ∩
Use when you want everything EXCEPT the elements in the given set (relative to Universal set). Example: A' = U - A
Use when you want elements in either set but NOT in both. Formula: A Δ B = (A - B) ∪ (B - A) = (A ∪ B) - (A ∩ B)
Use when listing all possible subsets of set A. If |A| = n, then |P(A)| = 2^n
Use as the reference set containing all possible elements under consideration. Everything else is measured relative to U.
Use to represent a set with NO elements. Zero, nothing, zilch. Also called null set.

visualTips.visualization: Draw two overlapping circles in a rectangle (Universal set). Shade BOTH entire circles completely. The entire shaded area = union. Think: Fill both cups to the brim.
Use the same Venn diagram. Only shade the OVERLAP region where both circles meet. That's the intersection - the common ground.
Shade EVERYTHING outside circle A but inside the rectangle (Universal set). It's the 'shadow' or 'reflection' of A - everything A is not.
Shade circle A but NOT the part that overlaps with B. Imagine cutting away the overlapping portion from A. What remains is A - B.
Shade both circles EXCEPT their overlap. It's like taking both and removing the common part - you get the 'exclusive OR' area.
Draw union with a line through it (complement). Break the line at the join - union becomes intersection. It's like a factory production line where complement 'breaks and transforms' the operation.

A ∩ (B ∪ C) = (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C). Draw three overlapping circles. First shade B ∪ C (both B and C), then intersect with A. You'll see it covers the same area as shading A∩B and A∩C separately and combining them.

analogies.analogy: Think of two different friend groups - Group A has friends {Alice, Bob} and Group B has {Bob, Charlie}. Union is inviting EVERYONE to the party: {Alice, Bob, Charlie}. Bob comes once, not twice!
Same friend groups - Intersection asks: 'Who is friends with BOTH groups?' That's just Bob - the common friend. He's in both lists.
Your University has all courses as Universal Set. Your Complement courses are all the courses you're NOT taking this semester.
Your music playlist (A) minus your friend's playlist (B) = songs only YOU have, that your friend doesn't have. You remove the duplicates.
Two people trading unique cards - you give your exclusives to them, they give theirs to you. Neither of you keeps the cards you both have in common.
A vegetarian's meat menu - it's a set with absolutely nothing in it (∅ or {}).

You have 3 shirts {Red, Blue, Green}. Power set lists ALL possible outfits you can make: {}, {Red}, {Blue}, {Green}, {Red,Blue}, {Red,Green}, {Blue,Green}, {Red,Blue,Green}. That's 2³ = 8 combinations.

NOT (Monday OR Tuesday) is the same as (NOT Monday) AND (NOT Tuesday). It's like: 'People who are not free on Monday or Tuesday' = 'People who are not free on Monday AND not free on Tuesday.' The negation distributes and flips the operation.
